construct_name: Soap Note: Financial Advisor Client Summary
construct_description: Give a verbal summary of a client's financial situation to a senior advisor who is taking over the account. Cover who the client is, the full financial snapshot, the key risks and gaps you've identified, and your priority recommendations.

scene.title: Client Account Handoff — Sandra Reyes
scene.context: You are a junior financial advisor handing off client Sandra Reyes to a senior advisor who will manage her account going forward. The senior advisor has her file open but hasn't reviewed it yet. You have about 2–3 minutes to walk through the key points. Don't just recite facts — flag the risks you see and give your read on what needs to happen first. The senior advisor will take it from there.
scene.urgency_label: Account handoff — senior advisor taking over

reference_sections.label: Client Profile
reference_sections.content_html:
NameSandra Reyes
Age / Sex54-year-old female
OccupationPublic school teacher
Retirement target11 years (age 65)
Marital statusDivorced, two adult children

reference_sections.label: Financial Snapshot
reference_sections.content_html:
Asset / LiabilityAmountNotes
403(b) retirement$187,00090% equities ⚠
Emergency fund$12,000~3 months expenses
Credit card debt($34,000)3 cards, 18–24% APR ⚠
Primary residence~$368,000 value$148K mortgage, ~$220K equity
Life insurance$50,000Employer only — no individual policy ⚠
Will / estate plan—None in place ⚠

reference_sections.label: Identified Risks
reference_sections.content_html:
• Allocation risk: 90% equities at 54 is too aggressive — a market downturn in the next 5 years could devastate retirement assets with insufficient time to recover
  • Debt drag: 18–24% APR credit card debt will compound faster than almost any investment return; carrying this into retirement is a serious risk
  • Retirement shortfall: $187K at 54 is significantly below typical benchmarks for her income level and timeline; needs accelerated contributions
  • Insurance gap: $50K employer life insurance may evaporate at retirement; no individual coverage for income replacement or mortgage protection
  • Estate vulnerability: No will or beneficiary review — assets could be distributed contrary to her wishes

reference_sections.label: Priority Recommendations
reference_sections.content_html:
1. Rebalance 403(b) — shift toward age-appropriate allocation (e.g., 60/40 or target-date fund)
  2. Aggressive debt paydown — avalanche method on the highest-APR card first; consider a balance transfer to lower-rate card
  3. Increase retirement contributions — maximize catch-up contributions (age 50+ eligible)
  4. Insurance review — quote individual term or permanent life policy before retirement
  5. Estate planning — will, healthcare proxy, beneficiary designations

sections.label: Who
sections.prompt: Who is the client? Name, age, profession, how far from retirement?

sections.label: Financial Snapshot
sections.prompt: Key numbers: retirement savings, debt, emergency fund, real estate, insurance.

sections.label: Risks / Gaps
sections.prompt: What are the major concerns you've identified? Be specific — name the issue and why it matters.

sections.label: Priority Recommendations
sections.prompt: What should the senior advisor focus on first? Give at least 2 concrete next steps.

required_elements.label: Client identification and retirement timeline
required_elements.teaching_point: Name, age, occupation, and retirement timeline belong in the first sentence. 'Sandra Reyes, 54-year-old teacher, 11 years from retirement' gives the senior advisor the lens through which to evaluate everything else you're about to say.

required_elements.label: Retirement savings and allocation concern
required_elements.teaching_point: The dollar amount and the allocation are both critical. $187K alone sounds okay; $187K in 90% equities at 54 is a risk flag. The senior advisor needs both data points — the number and the context — to understand the urgency of a rebalance conversation.

required_elements.label: Credit card debt amount and APR
required_elements.teaching_point: High-interest debt is often the highest-priority financial issue — it compounds at 18–24% while investments rarely earn that reliably. Naming the amount and the APR together conveys the urgency. 'She's carrying $34K in credit card debt at up to 24% APR' is a sentence that creates urgency.

required_elements.label: Emergency fund status
required_elements.teaching_point: Three months of reserves at 54 is thin — especially with high-interest debt and an underfunded retirement. Acknowledging the emergency fund status shows comprehensive review; it's easy to overlook the positives and the gaps that aren't dramatic but still matter.

required_elements.label: Home equity and insurance gap
required_elements.teaching_point: Home equity ($220K) is her largest asset and worth noting as a potential retirement resource. The $50K employer life insurance is a significant gap — it disappears at retirement and likely doesn't cover income replacement or the mortgage. Both belong in the snapshot.

required_elements.label: Priority recommendations (at least 2 concrete steps)
required_elements.teaching_point: Ending with concrete next steps is what separates a summary from an analysis. 'Rebalance the 403(b) and attack the credit card debt first' gives the senior advisor an agenda for the first client meeting. Vague recommendations like 'review her situation' don't help anyone take action.

performance_labels.label: Exemplary
performance_labels.description: All key numbers cited. Allocation risk, debt APR, and insurance gap all flagged. At least 2 priority recommendations with rationale. Senior advisor can walk into the client meeting prepared.

performance_labels.label: Proficient
performance_labels.description: Strong overview with most key figures. May be missing one data point (e.g., APR not mentioned, or estate planning gap skipped).

performance_labels.label: Developing
performance_labels.description: Covers client ID and retirement savings but missing debt detail or recommendations are vague. Senior advisor would need to re-review the file.

performance_labels.label: Beginning
performance_labels.description: Some figures mentioned but no risk framing. Reads like a recitation of numbers without clinical/advisory judgment.

performance_labels.label: Insufficient
performance_labels.description: Summary does not give the senior advisor enough to proceed with the client relationship.
